title: SQL Transactions & ACID Properties
description: Transaction management, ACID properties, isolation levels, and concurrency control.

questions.text: Which SQL statement marks the beginning of an explicit transaction?

options.text: START WORK
BEGIN TRANSACTION
OPEN TRANSACTION
INIT TRANSACTION

questions.text: What does COMMIT do in a transaction?

options.text: Undoes all changes made in the current transaction
Permanently saves all changes made in the current transaction to the database
Pauses the transaction
Creates a savepoint in the transaction

questions.text: What does ROLLBACK do?

options.text: Saves all changes permanently
Undoes all changes made since the last COMMIT or BEGIN TRANSACTION
Starts a new transaction
Locks a table

questions.text: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?

options.text: Accuracy, Concurrency, Isolation, Durability
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Atomicity, Concurrency, Integrity, Durability
Availability, Consistency, Isolation, Durability

questions.text: What does Atomicity in ACID mean?

options.text: The transaction runs on a single CPU core
All operations in a transaction are treated as a single unit — either all succeed or all are rolled back
The database stores data in atomic units of storage
The transaction cannot be split into smaller transactions

questions.text: What does Consistency in ACID mean?

options.text: All transactions run at the same speed
A transaction brings the database from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules and constraints
Data is consistent across all replicas
Queries always return the same results

questions.text: What does Durability in ACID guarantee?

options.text: The transaction will complete in a fixed time
Once committed, the transaction's changes are permanently saved even in case of system failure
The database will never go offline
Transactions will always succeed

questions.text: Which isolation level allows a transaction to read data that has been modified but not yet committed by another transaction?

options.text: READ COMMITTED
REPEATABLE READ
READ UNCOMMITTED
SERIALIZABLE

questions.text: What is a dirty read?

options.text: Reading data from a corrupted table
Reading uncommitted data from another transaction that may later be rolled back
Reading NULL values from a column
Reading data without proper permissions

questions.text: What is a non-repeatable read?

options.text: When the same query returns different results within the same transaction due to another transaction modifying the data
When a query returns no results
When a query cannot be executed due to a lock
When a transaction reads its own uncommitted changes

questions.text: What is a phantom read?

options.text: When a read sees data that has been physically deleted
When a transaction re-executes a query and finds new rows that were inserted by another committed transaction
When a read returns NULL for all columns
When a query sees its own uncommitted inserts

questions.text: Which isolation level prevents dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, AND phantom reads?

options.text: READ UNCOMMITTED
READ COMMITTED
REPEATABLE READ
SERIALIZABLE

questions.text: What is the default isolation level in SQL Server?

options.text: READ UNCOMMITTED
READ COMMITTED
REPEATABLE READ
SERIALIZABLE

questions.text: What is a deadlock in SQL?

options.text: When a query runs indefinitely due to a missing index
When two or more transactions each hold a lock the other needs, creating a circular wait that can never resolve
When a transaction is waiting for a lock for more than 30 seconds
When too many connections are open simultaneously

questions.text: How does SQL Server resolve a deadlock?

options.text: It waits indefinitely until one transaction completes
It automatically selects one transaction as the deadlock victim, rolls it back, and allows the other to proceed
It terminates both transactions
It escalates to a table lock

questions.text: What does a SAVEPOINT allow in a transaction?

options.text: Committing part of the transaction
Rolling back to a specific point within the transaction without rolling back the entire transaction
Saving the transaction to disk immediately
Starting a nested transaction

questions.text: What is the correct syntax to create a savepoint in SQL Server?

options.text: SAVEPOINT MySavepoint
SAVE TRANSACTION MySavepoint
CREATE SAVEPOINT MySavepoint
MARK TRANSACTION MySavepoint

questions.text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?

options.text: Optimistic locks data immediately; pessimistic checks for conflicts at commit time
Optimistic assumes no conflicts and checks at commit time; pessimistic locks data immediately to prevent conflicts
They are the same strategy with different names
Optimistic locking is only for reads; pessimistic only for writes

questions.text: What does the NOLOCK hint do in SQL Server? Example: SELECT * FROM Orders WITH (NOLOCK)

options.text: Prevents any locks from being taken, allowing dirty reads (READ UNCOMMITTED)
Prevents other transactions from reading the data
Acquires an exclusive lock on the table
Disables all locking for the entire session

questions.text: What does SET XACT_ABORT ON do?

options.text: Prevents transactions from starting
Automatically rolls back the current transaction when a statement raises an error
Aborts the connection when a transaction fails
Commits the transaction automatically after each statement

questions.text: What is an implicit transaction?

options.text: A transaction that is explicitly started with BEGIN TRANSACTION
A transaction automatically started by the database when executing a DML statement, committed or rolled back automatically
A transaction that spans multiple sessions
A transaction that cannot be rolled back

questions.text: Which strategy helps avoid deadlocks?

options.text: Always access tables in a different order in each transaction
Access tables in the same consistent order in all transactions and keep transactions short
Use READ UNCOMMITTED for all queries
Avoid using transactions altogether

questions.text: What does the Isolation property in ACID guarantee?

options.text: Transactions are always committed in the order they started
Concurrent transactions execute as if they were serial — intermediate states are not visible to other transactions
Each transaction runs on isolated hardware
Transactions cannot read each other's data under any circumstances

questions.text: In SQL Server, what does @@TRANCOUNT represent?

options.text: The number of rows affected by the last transaction
The number of active (open) transactions in the current session
The total number of committed transactions
The transaction isolation level as a number

questions.text: Which isolation level prevents dirty reads but still allows non-repeatable reads?

options.text: READ UNCOMMITTED
READ COMMITTED
REPEATABLE READ
SERIALIZABLE

questions.text: What happens when you issue ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT (or ROLLBACK TRANSACTION savepointname in SQL Server)?

options.text: The entire transaction is rolled back
Only the work done after the savepoint is undone; work before the savepoint remains
The savepoint is committed
A new transaction is started

questions.text: What type of lock does a SELECT statement typically acquire under READ COMMITTED?

options.text: Exclusive (X) lock
Shared (S) lock, released after each row/page is read
Update (U) lock
No lock at all

questions.text: READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT ISOLATION (RCSI) in SQL Server — what does it use instead of locks for reads?

options.text: Exclusive locks on all pages
Row versioning stored in tempdb to provide consistent reads without locking
Cached query results
A shadow copy of the database

questions.text: Which statement is TRUE about nested transactions in SQL Server?

options.text: SQL Server supports true nested transactions where inner COMMITs are independent
SQL Server does not support true nested transactions — only the outermost COMMIT saves data; inner COMMITs only decrease @@TRANCOUNT
Nested transactions always cause deadlocks
Each BEGIN TRANSACTION creates a completely independent transaction

questions.text: What is the purpose of using TRY...CATCH with transactions?

options.text: To speed up transaction commit
To catch errors and roll back the transaction gracefully instead of leaving it in an open, inconsistent state
To enable automatic commits after each statement
To log the transaction to a file
